title: T-Cell Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management
content: Retinoids help through their antiproliferative and antiapoptotic properties. Commonly used topical regimens are bexarotene (FDA-approved) and tazarotene. Severe hyperlipidemia and central hypothyroidism are side effects of bexarotene. Systemic retinoids include acitretin, isotretinoin, and bexarotene. Topical chemotherapy commonly used are mechlorethamine (nitrogen mustard), an alkylating agent, and carmustine. Systemic chemotherapy commonly used is methotrexate, pralatrexate, chlorambucil, gemcitabine, pegylated doxorubicin, and antimetabolite like fludarabine phosphate, 2-deoxycoformycin. PUVA inhibits DNA and RNA synthesis, but it will affect both normal and neoplastic cells; thus, the side effect profile is secondary malignancies of the skin. Histone deacetylase inhibitors (HDACi) are the newer group of therapeutic options available for the treatment of CTCL, which have both transcription-dependent and independent mechanisms of action for the expression of tumor suppressor genes. Vorinostat and romidepsin are FDA-approved, with an overall response rate of almost 50% with a tolerable side effect profile, the prominent one being cardiac arrhythmias from QTc prolongation. Targeted therapies include imiquimod (Toll-like receptor agonist), denileukin diftitox (recombinant fusion protein), alemtuzumab (a monoclonal antibody against CD52), and everolimus ( mTOR pathway target). Agents that are still undertrial are proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib. Allogenic stem cell transplant is used in advanced stages, usually appropriate for young patients with relapsing disease. [9]

title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Pathophysiology
content: B Cells abnormalities: The number of B cells is normal in the majority of patients, but many of them have reduced percentages of isotype-switched memory B cells capable of producing immunoglobin isotypes that are critical toward antibody response recall. The abnormalities that appear to occur in the bone marrow include aberrant gene rearrangement, decreased V gene replacements, reduced diversity of the naïve B cell repertoire, impaired somatic hypermutation, and abnormal expansion of unmutated B cell clones. [13] Toll-like receptor signaling: B cell maturation is defective, and the action of toll-like receptors 7 and 9 (TLR7 and TLR9) becomes impaired because of a yet unknown mechanism. Defective signaling of TLR7 and TLR9 in B cells and plasmacytoid dendritic cells, along with deficient IFN-a, impairs B cell functions and prevents TLR-mediated augmentation of humoral immunity in vivo. [14] [15] The binding of viral RNA to TLR7 or bacterial DNA to TLR9 leads to B cell activation, cytokine secretion, proliferation, and survival; in CVID, the loss of these stimuli functions may contribute to the B cell defects mentioned. [16] [17] Memory B cells: determining the number of isotype-switched memory B cells is of utmost interest because it provides information about the immaturity of the B cell, relates to the numbers of plasma cells in the bone marrow, and gives information about the possible clinical outcomes. [18] Various studies tried to classify CVID patients based on numbers of isotype-switched memory B cells [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] : Patients that switched CD27+IgM-IgD- memory B cells had a higher prevalence of splenomegaly and autoimmune cytopenias. This proposed a fast and reliable new classification for CVID patients with flow cytometric quantification of class-switched memory and immature B cells. Certain disorders were more prevalent in patients with the lowest count of memory B cells (splenomegaly, lymphoid proliferation, granulomatous disease). Lower levels of IgM memory B cells were associated with chronic pulmonary disease (bronchiectasis). Research has determined that the percentages of switched memory B cells changed in some patients over time. Based on these findings, the suggestion is that immunologic characteristics have to be evaluated several times after diagnosis. [24] Decreased T lymphocyte proliferation to mitogens and antigens, [1] reduced T regulatory cells, [25] T cell receptor excision circles that suggest thymic dysregulation, [26] defective T cell receptor signal transduction. Decrease in CD4+ cell count or increase in CD8+ cell count, altering the CD4/CD8 ratio. [27] Increase in CD26 and CD30 levels (the last ones associated with splenomegaly and malignancy) [28] . The minor capacity of dendritic cells to secrete interleukin-12. [29] A drop of plasma cells in the gastrointestinal tract and bone marrow. [18]

title: Imiquimod -- Mechanism of Action
content: Imiquimod is an immune response modifier of the imidazoquinolinamines drug class that induces the production of various cytokines, such as Interferon-alpha, Interferon-gamma, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and interleukin-8. [10] [11] Imiquimod activates the innate and adaptive immune responses via binding to Toll-like receptor 7 and activation of nuclear factor kappa-B with the resultant cytokine release and recruitment of plasmacytoid predendritic cells to the site of medication application with subsequent release of Interferon-alpha and a Th1 response. [12] [13] [14] [15] In addition to its anti-viral activity, imiquimod induces apoptosis of skin cancer cells and has demonstrated anti-tumoral activity. [16]

content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
content: Leukemia (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) is a blood‑cancer disease that is treated with a wide spectrum of antineoplastic agents, many of which belong to the heterogeneous pharmacologic class “antitumor substances” (antitumorózní látky). Cytarabine, irinotecan hydrochloride, methotrexát, aminopterin, azacytidin, busulfan, floxuridin, fluorouracil, leukovorin, mitobronitol, nafoxidin, pipobroman, poly A‑U and the BCG vaccine are listed as individual antitumor substances, reflecting conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy, immunomodulatory biologics and vaccine‑based strategies. Corticosteroid derivatives—including dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate and prednisolone tebutate—are also classified as “is a” members of the antitumor group, indicating their dual role as anti‑inflammatory agents and components of cancer therapy. Methotrexát is additionally identified as a non‑steroidal abortifacient, illustrating overlapping pharmacologic classifications. Viral agents such as human herpesvirus 1 (lidský herpesvirus 1) and the oncolytic virus therapy talimogene laherparevvec are incorporated within the antitumor category, yet both are contraindicated in patients with leukemia, underscoring safety considerations specific to this malignancy. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that the management of leukemia involves diverse therapeutic modalities—including traditional chemotherapeutics, targeted viral therapies, corticosteroids, and vaccine‑based agents—while requiring careful attention to drug classifications and disease‑specific contraindications.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 798
content: BCG vaccine, aminopterin, azacytidine, busulfan, floxuridine, fluorouracil, leukovorin, melphalan, mitobronitol, nafoxidine, and poly A‑U are all classified as antitumor substances. These agents represent a diverse group of chemotherapeutic and immunotherapeutic compounds used in cancer treatment. BCG vaccine functions as an immunotherapy that stimulates the immune system against tumor cells. Aminopterin is an antifolate that interferes with DNA synthesis. Azacytidine is a nucleoside analog that incorporates into RNA and DNA, leading to hypomethylation and cytotoxicity. Busulfan is an alkylating agent that cross‑links DNA strands. Floxuridine and fluorouracil are pyrimidine analogs that inhibit thymidylate synthase, disrupting DNA synthesis. Leukovorin (folinic acid) is often used to modulate the toxicity of fluoropyrimidines and enhance their efficacy. Melphalan is another alkylating agent used in multiple myeloma. Mitobronitol is a nitrosourea derivative with alkylating activity. Nafoxidine is a selective estrogen‑receptor modulator with antineoplastic properties. Poly A‑U, a synthetic double‑stranded RNA, has been investigated for its ability to induce interferon production and antitumor effects. Collectively, these agents belong to the broader pharmacological category of nafazetrom, indicating that they share a common therapeutic purpose of targeting malignant cells. Understanding the relationships among these antitumor substances helps clinicians select appropriate regimens and anticipate synergistic or antagonistic interactions in oncology care.

Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.
